content.text: SDG 3 goal: Ensure healthy lives and well-being for all ages​
Target 3.8: Achieve universal health coverage, including financial
risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and
access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines
and vaccines for all​
Without UHC → fragmented progress and inequity​
Maternal and child health: core outcomes reflecting system strength

content.title: UHC Service Coverage
content.title_scene_1: UCH Service Coverage Index by regions
content.title_scene_2: UCH Service Coverage Index by sub-components

scenes.text: [emphasis: The global UHC SCI score ]steadily increased from 45 to 68 between 2000 and 2021, but the progress has slowed since 2015
[emphasis: The UHC Service Coverage Index] is defined as coverage of essential services (including RMNCH, infectious diseases, NCDs, capacity & access).​

content.text: Inequalities in health service coverage remain despite global progress​
Maternal & child health outcomes in the UHC framework
Target 3.1: By 2030, reduce the global maternal mortality ratio to less than 70
per 100,000 live births​
Target 3.2: By 2030, end preventable deaths of newborns and children under 5
years of age, with all countries aiming to reduce neonatal mortality to at least
as low as 12 per 1,000 live births and under-5 mortality to at least as low as 25
per 1,000 live births
Linked to UHC monitoring: coverage of maternal and child health services is a key sub-index in the UHC framework.​
Proof of progress: falling mortality indicates better access to life-saving care.​
System quality: high deaths reveal gaps in timely, affordable, quality care​
Interlinked outcomes: maternal and child survival are closely connected — the loss of a
mother greatly increases a child’s risk of dying.
Levels and trends in maternal mortality​

content.title: Levels and trends in maternal mortality

scenes.text: Overall [emphasis: maternal mortality​ rate] of 197 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births in 2023​. Despite major reductions since 2000, the pace of decline has stagnated in recent years, with a temporary uptick duringCOVID-19 before returning to previous trends.[emphasis: ]

The global [emphasis: under-5 mortality rate] has halved from 77 in 2000 to 37 deaths per 1,000 live births in 2023, saving millions of young lives. ​ Despite progress, Sub-Saharan Africa still records mortality rates (68) more than 10 times higher than high-income countries (5). Progress in reducing neonatal deaths has been slower — 17 per 1,000 live births in 2023, making newborns an increasing share of child mortality.​[emphasis: ]
In terms of the [emphasis: neonatal mortality rate ]South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa still face the highest burdens, though both regions show steady improvement.

content.text: Progress
While some countries like Sierra Leone and Ethiopia in Sub-Saharan Africa made
faster progress, many countries in Latin America & the Caribbean regressedin reducing maternal mortality.

scenes.text: While some countries like Sierra Leone and Ethiopia in Sub-Saharan Africa made faster progress, many countries in Latin America & the Caribbean regressed in reducing maternal mortality.

Rwanda and Malawi in Sub-Saharan Africa, along with several countries in Asia and the Pacific, advanced quickly, but many Sub-Saharan African countries still show slower declines in under-5 mortality.
Cambodia, China, and India in Asia and the Pacific and South Asia made faster gains, yet Sub-Saharan Africa shows uneven improvements in reducing

content.text: What drives progress?​
Common Factors Behind Progress – studies to be cited​
Skilled birth attendance​
RMNCH service coverage​
Health workforce & infrastructure​
Financial protection → reduces catastrophic spending
RMNCH outcomes and UHC
RMNCH service is a core component of the UHC SCI.​
Countries that increased overall UHC (e.g., Sierra Leone, Ethiopia, Bangladesh) also show progress in maternal mortality reduction.​
Service coverage helps ensure essential RMNCH care is available and accessible.

content.text: Countries that achieved faster reductions in maternal mortality also tended to record
improvements in the RMNCH sub-index.

content.text: Conclusion
UHC is critical to sustaining maternal and child health gains and ensuring
equity​
Integrating health service and financing policies is key to expanding both coverage and affordability​
Monitoring SDG 3.1, 3.2, and 3.8 together keeps attention on survival, access, and protection—and accelerates progress to 2030
